title: Fennel infusion for menstrual cramps
subtitle: An antispasmodic cup for period pain
excerpt: A hot infusion of crushed fennel seeds for menstrual cramping. Fennel's antispasmodic action on smooth muscle, the same action that eases gut cramps, also applies to the uterine muscle. One of the oldest traditional herbs for dysmenorrhoea in the western and Mediterranean tradition.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), fennel entry, carminative and antispasmodic action, emmenagogue properties. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), fennel for female complaints. EMA HMPC monograph on Foeniculum vulgare (2008), acknowledges traditional use for mild spasmodic gastrointestinal complaints; the dysmenorrhoea application is traditional rather than EMA-confirmed at this time. Ozgoli G. et al. (2007), comparison of fennel with mefenamic acid for primary dysmenorrhoea, small Iranian RCT finding comparable efficacy at 30-minute onset.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Crush seeds just before brewing, the volatile oils that carry the antispasmodic action dissipate quickly once the seed is broken.

recipeTools.notes: For lightly crushing the seeds. A rolling pin works.

glossaryTerms.definition: The medical term for painful periods, cramping pain caused by uterine muscle contractions during menstruation.
In the uterine context: a herb that reduces the intensity of smooth-muscle spasm in the uterus, easing the cramping pain of dysmenorrhoea.

content.text: A cup of fennel seed tea for period cramps. The same
action that fennel exerts on the gut smooth muscle works on the uterine muscle, reducing the intensity of the cramping contractions responsible for

. Maud Grieve notes fennel's traditional use for female complaints and its emmenagogue properties; a small Iranian randomised trial (Ozgoli et al., 2007) found fennel extract comparable to mefenamic acid for primary dysmenorrhoea at 30-minute onset. The preparation is the same as the digestive fennel cup: a teaspoon of lightly crushed fennel seeds in a lidded pot, 10 minutes' steep, drunk slowly at the first sign of cramping.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy: fennel has weak oestrogenic activity and has traditionally been classified as an emmenagogue at therapeutic doses. A cup of fennel tea after a meal is at the low end of the dose range; regular daily therapeutic-strength fennel preparations during pregnancy are best avoided without herbalist advice. Culinary use in cooking is unproblematic.

Allergy: fennel is in the Apiaceae family (carrot, celery, parsley, coriander). Avoid if you have a known allergy to any of these plants.

Hormone-sensitive conditions: fennel's weak oestrogenic properties mean that regular high-dose fennel preparations may not be appropriate for people with oestrogen-sensitive conditions (oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids). Seek herbal or medical advice before regular therapeutic use.

See your doctor for period pain that is getting progressively worse over several cycles, periods that are unusually heavy, pain that occurs outside of the period, or pain that does not respond to standard analgesics. These can be symptoms of endometriosis, fibroids, or other conditions that need diagnosis.

items.prepNote: about 1 teaspoon, lightly crush just before brewing

content.text: Lightly crush {{fennel-seeds}} of fennel seeds in a pestle and mortar, three or four firm presses to crack the seed coat. Place in the teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over and lid immediately. Steep for 10 minutes. Strain and drink slowly, ideally with a hot water bottle against the lower abdomen, the combination of internal antispasmodic action and external heat works faster than either alone.

One cup at the first sign of cramping. Up to three cups over the first 24 hours of painful cramping, morning, midday, and evening. Continue for the first two to three days of the period if cramping is persistent. For the fennel cup to be most effective, start at the very beginning of cramping rather than waiting until the pain is severe.
When NOT to use this remedy

Avoid therapeutic-dose fennel preparations in pregnancy. Skip if you have a known Apiaceae allergy. Discuss with a herbalist or gynaecologist if you have an oestrogen-sensitive condition. See your doctor for progressive worsening of period pain, unusually heavy bleeding, or pain outside of the menstrual period.
